content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 25, 2011

Daniel M. Gounder
Employment Development Department
722 Capitol Mall, MIC 62-5
Sacramento, CA 94814

Marie Ziegler
Employment Development Department
722 Capitol Mall, MIC 62-5
Sacramento, CA 94814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our file No. A-11-083

Dear Mr. Gounder and Ms. Ziegler:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Does the Act require you to report as a gift the use of the public area at the Radisson
Hotel bar for no charge?

CONCLUSION

No. Because the Radisson Hotel offers their bar area to anyone who asks, regardless of
his or her official position, for no charge, the use of the space is exempted from the Act's
definition of "gift."

FACTS

You are both Conference/Event Specialists in the Centralized Conference Coordination
Unit of the State Employment Development Department (EDD). You both file Statements of
Economic Interests based on your designated positions. Your responsibilities include securing
hotels or other venues, negotiating contracts, and providing analyses to your director to assist in
decision-making related to events and conferences.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-11-083
Page No. 2


In February 2011, you planned a retirement party for an individual who retired from your
department. The party was held at the Radisson Hotel in Sacramento in its bar/lounge area. The
area was not cordoned off, but was available to the public during your event. Guests purchased
their own food and beverages and contributed a small amount to a gift for the retiree.

On inquiry, the Radisson Hotel informed you that it happily allows any group to use its
bar/lounge space at no charge. In the Radisson's view, such events are opportunities to gain
business it would not otherwise have. The venue is open to the public, whether a group is
occupying the space or not.

You now ask whether the use of the space at the Radisson Hotel is a reportable gift.

ANALYSIS

The Gift Disclosure Provisions

Public officials, other than those covered by Section 87200, who make, participate in
making, or use their official position to influence governmental decisions, must disclose certain
economic interests as required by their respective agency's conflict of interest codes. (Sections
87300, 87302.) Referred to as "designated employees," they report the receipt of gifts, valued at
$50 or more in the aggregate in a calendar year from a single source if the gift is from a source
required to be disclosed under the agency conflict of interest code. (Sections 82030; 87302.)
Gifts valued at $420 or more in the aggregate in a calendar year from a single source are
prohibited. (Section 89503(c).)

Both of you have stated that you are designated employees under your conflict of interest
code. The gift limits and reporting requirements therefore apply to both of you, per the
restrictions in your code.

Discounts as Gifts

Section 82028(a) defines a "gift" as:

"Any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to
the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not
received and includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything
of value unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular course
of business to members of the public without regard to official
status."

Thus, a discount is a gift, unless the discount is made in the regular course of business, to
members of the public, without regard to official status. We construe a 100 percent discount, in
this case, free use of the premises, to be included within the meaning of a discount. A gift does
not include, however, "a discount made in the regular course of business to members of the
public without regard to official status." (Section82028(a).) The Radisson "discounts" its

File No. A-11-083
Page No. 3

bar/lounge area by providing free use for any group who wishes to use it. The Radisson does not
charge for the space and does not section off the space for private events. The space is available
to the general public in the regular course of business. Indeed, the Radisson stated that it was in
its interest to offer the space for the retirement party as the party would provide additional clients
(and therefore additional income) at the bar.

Based on your facts, there is no reportable gift under the Act.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel

By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division

HMR:jgl

sections.question:
Does the Act require you to report as a gift the use of the public area at the Radisson 
Hotel bar for no charge?
sections.conclusion:
No. Because the Radisson Hotel offers their bar area to anyone who asks, regardless of 
his or her official position, for no charge, the use of the space is exempted from the Act's 
definition of 'gift.'

QUESTION: Does the Act require you to report as a gift the use of the public area at the Radisson 
Hotel bar for no charge?

CONCLUSION: No. Because the Radisson Hotel offers their bar area to anyone who asks, regardless of 
his or her official position, for no charge, the use of the space is exempted from the Act's 
definition of 'gift.'
